questionText: Let $\theta = 36^\circ$. By using the fact that $3\theta + 2\theta = 180^\circ$, together with the triple- and double-angle formulae for cosine, show that $\cos 36^\circ = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{4}$.

steps.explanation: Because five copies of $36^\circ$ make $180^\circ$, we can relate the triple angle $3\theta$ to the double angle $2\theta$, which is the engine of the whole derivation.
Rearranging $3\theta + 2\theta = 180^\circ$ expresses $3\theta$ as a supplement of $2\theta$, which we can feed into the cosine of a supplement.
Cosine of a supplement flips sign, so $\cos 3\theta = -\cos 2\theta$. This single equation links the triple and double angles of $\theta$.
This identity follows from the compound-angle formulae (expanding $\cos(2\theta+\theta)$) and lets us write $\cos 3\theta$ in terms of $\cos\theta$.
Choosing the all-cosine form of $\cos 2\theta$ keeps the whole equation in a single variable, $\cos\theta$.
Naming $\cos 36^\circ$ as $c$ turns the trigonometric equation into an ordinary polynomial equation in $c$.
Replacing $\cos 3\theta$ and $\cos 2\theta$ with their formulae in $\cos 3\theta = -\cos 2\theta$ produces a cubic equation for $c$.
Distributing the minus sign prepares the equation for collecting all terms on one side.
Bringing every term to the left gives a cubic whose roots include $\cos 36^\circ$.
Testing $c=-1$ gives zero, so $(c+1)$ is a factor. This root corresponds to $\cos 180^\circ$, an unwanted solution we will discard.
Dividing out the known factor $(c+1)$ leaves a quadratic that contains the value we actually want.
Since $\cos 36^\circ$ is clearly not $-1$, the relevant solutions come from the quadratic factor.
The quadratic formula gives the two exact roots; the discriminant is $4 + 16 = 20$.
Simplifying $\sqrt{20}=2\sqrt{5}$ and cancelling a factor of $2$ gives the two candidate exact values.
Since $36^\circ$ is acute, its cosine is positive, so we reject the negative root and keep $\dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{4}$.
The exact value evaluates to about $0.8090$, exactly matching a calculator value of $\cos 36^\circ$, so the result is confirmed.

workedSolution.finalAnswer: Using $\cos 3\theta = -\cos 2\theta$ with $\theta=36^\circ$ leads to $4c^2 - 2c - 1 = 0$ where $c=\cos 36^\circ$; taking the positive root gives $\cos 36^\circ = \dfrac{1+\sqrt{5}}{4} \approx 0.809$.
